mappings.rationaleAI: Study of linear equations, linear maps, and matrices connects to calculatory aspects of algebra and algebraic notation (A.4). Solution of linear systems is central to numerical analysis of linear equations (C.4). Applications to mechanics (particles and systems) correspond to H.1, and applications to quantum theory correspond to H.8.

AI systems for learning, reasoning, perception, and decision-making directly address artificial intelligence within computer science (G.6). Strategic game-playing (AlphaGo, chess) and optimization connect to the theory of games and analysis of strategic conflict situations (E.1).

Comprehensive coverage of descriptive and inferential statistics including means, standard deviations, distributions (B.1.b), sampling and experimental design (B.1.a), hypothesis testing and confidence intervals (B.3), estimation techniques (B.2), and regression analysis for structure in data (B.4) maps across the full statistics outline (B).

History from abacus and mechanical calculators through analog and digital architectures covers analog computation and analogue computers (A.6, A.6.b) and digital computation including programmed machines (A.7, A.7.c). Hardware architecture including CPU, memory, and I/O corresponds to computer architecture (G.3).
Wave functions, probability amplitudes, uncertainty principle, superposition, and entanglement directly address the mathematical aspects of quantum mechanics (H.8).

Study of computation, information, and automation covering algorithms and data structures (G.1), theory of computation and abstract models (G.2), hardware design and computer architecture (G.3), and programming languages and software engineering (G.4) maps across the computer science outline (G).

Kolmogorov's axioms, sample spaces, events, conditional probability, and Bayes' theorem underpin statistical inference concepts (B.1). Random variables and mathematical description of experiments connect to B.1.a. Distributions and the Gaussian distribution match B.1.b. Probabilistic questions in stochastic processes relate to random effects in automata operation (D.3).

Natural numbers, integers, rationals, reals, and complex numbers, with coverage of numeral systems including Hindu-Arabic, the development of zero, and historical numerical notations directly address numerical notations (A.1), ancient numerical notations (A.1.b), and decimal notation and modern developments (A.1.c).

The Gaussian (normal) distribution and method of least squares connect directly to distribution functions (B.1.b) and estimation techniques (B.2). Gaussian curvature and Theorema Egregium in differential geometry relate to Riemannian geometry (H.7). Geodetic surveys and astronomical measurement connect to applications of geometry to celestial measurement (A.2.d).

Newtonian, Lagrangian, and Hamiltonian mechanics describing motion and equilibrium of macroscopic bodies under forces directly address the mathematical aspects of mechanics of particles and systems (H.1).
Finite rigorous sequences of instructions for solving problems, classified by design paradigm and formalized via Turing machines, directly address synthesis and analysis of algorithms (G.1).

Functions as mappings from domain to codomain, with properties including composition and extension, are central to approximation of functions including interpolation and least squares approximation (C.3).

Polynomial, linear, differential, and Diophantine equations connect to calculatory aspects of algebra (A.4) and algebraic notation (A.4.a). Solving equations of various types directly addresses solution of linear, nonlinear, and differential equations in numerical analysis (C.4).

Properties of space including distance, shape, and angle from Euclidean through differential and non-Euclidean geometries correspond to geometrical aids (A.2) and early applications of geometry (A.2.a). Non-Euclidean geometries developed by Gauss and Riemann directly address Riemannian geometry (H.7).

Standardization of mathematical notation (pi, e, i, f(x), sigma) and contributions to analysis connect to calculatory aspects of algebra and algebraic notation (A.4). Founding of graph theory relates to graphs and graphical procedures (A.5.b). Contributions to fluid dynamics correspond to mathematical aspects of fluid mechanics (H.2).

Laws of motion and universal gravitation establishing classical mechanics directly address mechanics of particles and systems (H.1). Co-development of infinitesimal calculus and contributions to numerical analysis connect to solution of equations (C.4) and numerical analysis broadly (C).

Special and general relativity establishing space and time as a four-dimensional continuum and gravitation as spacetime curvature directly address relativity theory (H.6). Contributions to statistical mechanics and Bose-Einstein statistics connect to statistical mechanics (H.4).

Formalization of algorithm and computation via the Turing machine founding theoretical computer science directly addresses theory of computation (G.2). The Turing machine as a foundational automaton connects to definition and development of automata theory (D.1). Cryptanalysis of Enigma relates to applications of information theory to cryptography (F.4).
Encryption, authentication, and the mathematical practice of securing communications directly address applications of information theory to cryptography (F.4).

Quantification of attributes enabling comparison against reference quantities, including the SI system and metrology, relates to numerical notations and units for counting or grouping (A.1). Historical unified standards and measurement instruments connect to instruments for observation and navigation (A.2.b).

Robot control systems integrating sensing and software (including AI) connect to control theory (E.4). Robots as an application domain for AI connect to artificial intelligence within computer science (G.6).

Force as a vector quantity in Newton's laws of motion, with applications to mechanics of particles including torque, friction, and centripetal forces, directly addresses mechanics of particles and systems (H.1).

Space as a three-dimensional continuum unified with time in four-dimensional spacetime under general relativity directly addresses relativity theory (H.6). Non-Euclidean curved geometries developed by Gauss and Riemann connect to Riemannian geometry (H.7).

Numerical representation of part-to-whole ratios as numerator over denominator, foundational to arithmetic and proportion, connects to numerical notations and units used for counting and grouping (A.1).

Elementary numerical operations across number systems, covering Hindu-Arabic and other numeral systems, correspond to numerical notations (A.1), ancient numerical notations from Egyptian and Sumerian calculation (A.1.b), and decimal notation (A.1.c). Historical computational arithmetic and mechanization relate to mathematical tables (A.5.a).

Spans prehistoric counting through Babylonian algebra, Egyptian practical mathematics, Greek axiomatic proof, Islamic algebra, and modern developments. Ancient numerical systems correspond to ancient numerical notations (A.1.b), early land measurement to early applications of geometry (A.2.a), and symbolic algebra development to calculatory aspects of algebra (A.4). Broadly covers mathematics as a calculatory science (A).

First systematic treatise on algebra presenting solutions of linear and quadratic equations directly addresses calculatory aspects of algebra (A.4) and algebraic notation (A.4.a). Introduction of the decimal positional number system to Western Europe connects to decimal notation and modern notational developments (A.1.c).
Fundamental natural science covering classical mechanics, electromagnetism, quantum mechanics, and relativity provides the physical context for mathematical aspects of physical theories (H).

Study of celestial objects using mathematics and physics, from planetary science to cosmology, directly connects to applications of geometry to celestial measurement (A.2.d). Observational astronomy historically drove development of instruments for observation and navigation (A.2.b).
Electric charge, electric fields, electric current, and electromagnetic induction unified through Maxwell's equations directly address the mathematical aspects of electromagnetic theory (H.5).

Maxwell's equations providing a complete classical description of electromagnetic fields, the Lorentz force, and electromagnetic wave prediction directly address the mathematical aspects of electromagnetic theory (H.5).

Numbers of the form a + bi with Euler's formula and the fundamental theorem of algebra connect to calculatory aspects of algebra (A.4). Applications in quantum mechanics and signal processing relate to mathematical aspects of quantum mechanics (H.8).

Euclidean 3-space with Cartesian, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates connecting to geometrical aids (A.2). Calculus in 3D including gradient, divergence, and curl applied to classical mechanics relates to mechanics of particles and systems (H.1). Generalization to 3-manifolds and spacetime connects to Riemannian geometry (H.7).

Newton's law of universal gravitation modeling force proportional to mass product directly addresses mechanics of particles and systems (H.1). Einstein's general relativity reinterpreting gravity as spacetime curvature connects to relativity theory as space and time as a four-dimensional continuum (H.6).

Linear momentum as a conserved quantity in Newtonian, Lagrangian, and Hamiltonian mechanics directly addresses mechanics of particles and systems (H.1). Momentum densities in fluid mechanics via Navier-Stokes equations connect to fluid mechanics (H.2). Momentum as a quantum mechanical operator related to position via the uncertainty principle addresses quantum mechanics (H.8).

Repeated multiplication extended to various exponents with inverse operations being roots and logarithms connects to calculatory aspects of algebra (A.4). The direct relationship between exponentiation and logarithms addresses logarithms (A.4.b).

Modeling the universe's large-scale structure using general relativity, including spacetime curvature, gravitational waves, and cosmic evolution, directly addresses relativity theory and space-time as a four-dimensional continuum (H.6).

Time as a fundamental dimension of spacetime in general relativity, covering special and general relativistic time dilation, directly addresses relativity theory and space and time as a four-dimensional continuum (H.6).

Continuous functions, limits, differentiation, integration, and differential equations underpin numerical analysis (C). Treatment of function approximation connects to approximation of functions (C.3). Differential equations connect to solution of differential equations (C.4).

Study of integers, arithmetic functions, divisibility, and congruences connects to calculatory aspects of algebra (A.4). Foundational role in public-key cryptography (RSA) directly relates to applications of information theory to cryptography (F.4).
